data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Draws Back
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 1.0 percentage points to 32.6%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the fourth time in six weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 4.6 percentage points to 23.9%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 70th time in 72 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 5.6 percentage points to 43.6%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 51st time in 53 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 6.5 percentage points to –11.0%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 38th time in 42 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how they view the current level of market concentration in mega-cap technology stocks.

Here is how they responded:

  • It’s a major risk that concerns me: 37.5%
  • It’s somewhat concerning, but manageable: 33.2%
  • It’s a natural result of strong business fundamentals: 13.6%
  • It creates good opportunities in other sectors: 13.2%
  • Not sure/no opinion: 2.1%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 32.6%, up 1.0 percentage points
  • Neutral: 23.9%, up 4.6 percentage points
  • Bearish: 43.6%, down 5.6 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.

If you want to become an effective manager of your own assets and achieve your financial goals, consider a risk-free 30-day Trial AAII Membership.

data.title: Lumexa Imaging Seeks IPO To Pay Down Debt
data.description:
Lumexa Is Producing Quality Growth

Lumexa Imaging Holdings, Inc. (LMRI) has filed to raise debt repayment capital in an IPO of its common stock, according to SEC S-1 registration information.

LMRI provides diagnostic imaging services to patients in the United States.

While the company's operating results have improved, the IPO’s proceeds will go to pay off debt, typical for a private equity-owned firm at IPO.

Many of these PE-owned firms have performed in a lackluster manner post-IPO, but perhaps Lumexa may break the pattern.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we learn more IPO details from management.

What Does Lumexa Do?

Raleigh, North Carolina-based Lumexa Imaging Holdings, Inc. was founded to develop a network of imaging diagnostic centers covering numerous US state geographies.

The firm was originally created as a joint venture between Charlotte Radiology and private equity firm Welsh, Carson, Anderson and Stowe [WCAS], and was renamed US Radiology Specialists. It was later rebranded as Lumexa Imaging in July 2025.

It has 99 consolidated locations, comprising either VIEs (Variable Interest Entities) or wholly-owned subsidiaries, with 48 and 51 locations, respectively, as of September 30, 2025.

There are also 85 joint venture locations with health systems, which are unconsolidated affiliates accounted for via the equity method.

Management is led by Chief Executive Officer Ms. Caitlin Zulla, who has been with the firm since January 2025 and was previously CEO of SCA Health, an operator of outpatient surgery locations.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • MRI imaging

  • CT imaging

  • Other services

Lumexa generates its revenue from sources according to the pie chart breakdown below:

For the nine months ended Sept. 30, 2025, advanced imaging represented 52% of its consolidated revenue and 63% of system-wide revenue.

A map of the company's footprint and system metrics is shown here:

Lumexa has booked fair market value investment of $1.5 billion as of September 30, 2025 from investors, including private equity firm Welsh, Carson, Anderson & Stowe and Charlotte Radiology Investors, LLC.

General & Administrative expenses as a percentage of total revenue have trended higher as revenues have grown, as the figures below indicate:

General & Administrative

Expenses vs. Revenue

Period

Percentage

Nine Mos. Ended Sept. 30, 2025

7.1%

Year Ended December 31, 2024

7.4%

Year Ended December 31, 2023

5.9%

(Source - SEC)

The General & Administrative efficiency multiple, i.e., how many dollars of additional revenue are produced from each dollar of General & Administrative expense, rose to 1.0x in the most recent reporting period, as shown in the table below:

General & Administrative

Efficiency Rate

Period

Multiple

Nine Mos. Ended Sept. 30, 2025

Year Ended December 31, 2024

(Source - SEC)

What Is Lumexa’s Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by Fortune Business Insights, the US market for diagnostic imaging services was an estimated $140 billion in 2024 and was projected to reach nearly $240 billion by 2032.

This represents a projected CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) of 7.0% from 2025 to 2032, a reasonably robust growth expectation given the already large size of the market.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are an aging population driving growing demand, coupled with improving technological offerings and a widening application space for medical imaging.

Also, the footprint of diagnostic centers is expected to drive convenience and related growth.

Declining reimbursement rates combined with rising equipment prices may place greater financial strains on smaller healthcare providers, potentially creating demand for lower-cost providers such as Lumexa.

Also, the graphic below illustrates the market dynamics for the US diagnostic imaging services market.

Major competitive vendors or industry players include the following firms:

  • RadNet (RDNT)

  • Akumin (OTCPK:AKUMQ)

  • Rayus Radiology

  • MedQuest Associates

  • LucidHealth

  • Radiology Partners

  • Envision Radiology

  • Capitol Imaging Services

The company markets its services via an in-house sales force of more than 120 sales representatives to physician groups and health systems in the US.

Lumexa seeks to position itself to insurance payors as a cost-effective alternative to hospital-based imaging.

Below is a thumbnail SWOT analysis for Lumexa:

Strengths

  • Large multi-state network of imaging centers, integrated technology platform, substantial network of subspecialized radiologists, longstanding joint venture relationships

Weaknesses

  • High fixed cost structure, heavy dependence on contracted radiology groups, meaningful indebtedness

Opportunities

  • Growing population of aged persons in the US, lower cost structure than hospital imaging, more joint ventures with health systems lacking imaging partners

Threats

  • Government or commercial payor reimbursement reduction risks, shortage of qualified radiologists, regulatory changes and high competition from hospitals or physician practices that acquire their own equipment

Lumexa’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Moderately growing topline revenue

  • Increasing gross profit and gross margin

  • Growing operating profit

  • A swing to slight cash used in operations

Below are major financial results from the firm’s current registration statement:

(Source - SEC)

As of September 30, 2025, Lumexa had $191,000 in cash and equivalents and $1.5 billion in total liabilities, of which debt totalled $1.2 billion.

The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended September 30, 2025, of $1.3 million.

The firm’s performance as a percentage of revenue is shown in the chart below:

The chart indicates that gross margin has improved materially in the first nine months of 2025, while sales & marketing expenses as a percentage of revenue have remained flat, indicating increasing operating leverage.

Lumexa’s IPO Plan

LMRI intends to sell shares of common stock in an IPO that may reach $200 million in size, not including the sale of customary underwriter options.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring shares of the IPO, at least as disclosed in the initial prospectus.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the normal and customary 180 days after the effective date of the prospectus.

According to Lumexa’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

We intend to use the net proceeds of this offering to pay down a portion of our outstanding borrowings under the Existing Term Loan and for working capital, capital expenditures and other general corporate purposes. At September 30, 2025, we had approximately $1.2 billion outstanding under the Existing Term Loan (which will mature in December 2027), which bears interest at a rate per annum equal to the secured overnight financing rate (“SOFR”), plus 4.75% (where the applicable SOFR rate has a 0.5% floor). As of September 30, 2025, the interest rate on the Existing Term Loan was 9.05%. The Refinancing Term Loan (as defined below) that we expect to incur under the New Credit Agreement following the completion of this offering is expected to be in the range of $800 million to $825 million.

(Source - SEC)

The firm’s presentation for its roadshow is not yet available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, leadership said the company had no legal exposure that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or operations.

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Barclays, J.P. Morgan, Jefferies, Deutsche Bank Securities, Wells Fargo Securities, Leerink Partners, William Blair, Capital One Securities, Fifth Third Securities, Raymond James, PNC Capital Markets LLC, Academy Securities, Loop Capital Markets and R. Seelaus & Co., LLC.

Lumexa Is Improving Operating Results

LMRI is seeking US public capital market investment to pay down its debt, likely incurred as a result of its relationship with its private equity firm investor WCAS.

The firm’s financials have produced slowly growing topline revenue, growing gross profit and gross margin, increasing operating profit and a swing to slight cash used in operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended September 30, 2025, was only $1.3 million.

General & Administrative expenses as a percentage of total revenue have moved higher as revenue has risen and its General & Administrative efficiency multiple rose to 1.0x in the most recent reporting period.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends and will likely retain all earnings and available cash for its growth initiatives and working capital needs.

LMRI’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent heavily on capital expenditures as a percentage of its operating cash flow.

The company did not pay any share-based compensation in recent years.

The firm’s growth strategy includes improving same-center growth, building new centers in new and existing geographies, new joint ventures with health systems, and select acquisitions of existing imaging center companies.

The market opportunity for providing diagnostic imaging services in the US is quite large and expected to grow at a reasonably strong rate of growth through 2032, on aging demographics, improving technologies and broadening applications, so the company has positive industry growth dynamics in its favor.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its ‘emerging growth company’ status, which allows leadership to disclose less information to shareholders.

LMRI has revenue concentration risks due to its operations covering only 13 states, and a substantial portion of revenue comes from the US federal government.

LMRI has produced improved operating results, and its revenue growth rate has also increased, but remains in the single digits.

With the IPO’s proceeds going to pay down debt, which is typical of a private equity firm-owned company at IPO, that's a less-than-optimal aspect of the IPO.

Many of these types of companies have performed disappointingly post-IPO, as they tend to be slow-growing, debt-heavy firms with little new resources with which to implement their growth plans.

When we learn management’s pricing and valuation assumptions, I’ll provide a final opinion.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced

data.title: Interest Rate Outlook: Will The BoC Be On Hold For All Of 2026?
data.description:
The Canadian economy faces plenty of challenges for 2026, but that doesn’t necessarily mean investors should be expecting more cuts from the Bank of Canada. Andrew Kelvin, Head of Canadian and Global Rates Strategy with TD Securities, joins MoneyTalk to discuss his expectations for the BOC as well as the latest 2026 Global Outlook report.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: While Canada's economy has performed better than we may have expected, given all these major trade barriers we've been facing, is that resilience going to continue into 2026? Joining us now to discuss is Andrew Kelvin, head of Canadian and global strategy with TD Securities.

Great to have you here, Andrew.

Andrew Kelvin: Thank you for having me. Always a pleasure.

Greg Bonnell: Alright. You got a new report out. We want to dig in. I think the Canadian component of this one, you guys titled "One Battle After Another." Of course, a lot of challenges. What's the base case now for 2026?

Andrew Kelvin: It remains a challenging environment in many respects. We still have the suboptimal trading relationship with the United States. But we actually think this is a year we can see Canada start to overcome some of the challenges in front of it.

We've seen the government come out with a reasonably expansive budget. It was not a maximal expansive budget that perhaps people expected. And I think really, really importantly, our assumption is that (a) trading conditions between Canada and the United States do not get worse; and (b) the impact from the change in trading conditions we've seen, that drag lessens over time, that we see that as something that was a bigger issue for 2025 growth than 2026 growth.

So if you layer in less drag from trade, more drag from fiscal-- more growth, sorry-- from fiscal stimulus, and hopefully, hopefully a candidate US-Mexico trade agreement renewal ahead of July 1, we think we can see accelerating growth over the course of the year, a little bit better than last year.

Greg Bonnell: Alright. So that's a nice base case for us heading to 2026. Let's start to break down those components. The current government managing to get its budget passed this week. How big of an impact on this economy are you expecting from that document?

Andrew Kelvin: So we think that the budget, with its aggregate new spending of about 1 percentage point worth of GDP per year, can boost GDP growth by-- over 2025 and 2026-- by about 1.4 percentage points, 1.25 if we want to round here, between 1.25 and 1.5. The critical thing is the government does seem to believe they can get the money out the door rather quickly, which means we can see a lot of that growth come in calendar 2026.

So we see that adding about a percentage point to Canadian growth next year, which is critical for Canada in the context of an economy where we have zero population growth and therefore quite low trend growth rates. That fiscal stimulus is really the thing that keeps us, we hope, comfortably away from having a 2026 recession conversation.

Greg Bonnell: Is this the kind of stimulus that has a multiplier effect? So it's one thing for the government to put $1 into the economy and get a $1 return. But we're hoping for more from this, I think.

Andrew Kelvin: We're hoping for more. I think it's honestly too early to say. If you think about some of those large-ticket items, there was a small income tax cut in there. That tends not to be something that has a large multiplier through the economy.

Given that it was a fairly broad tax cut, you usually see that money spent, but it's not something that tends to create productive investments. There's some new agencies where we've yet to see how effective they will be in incentivizing growth from the private sector. There are some depreciation measures that are designed to incentivize private sector growth. We'll have to see if those ones can deliver more than $1 out for every $1 that goes in.

The big question I have is there's quite a bit of money allocated to defense spending. Defense spending is going to have an import component to it. And how effective that is in driving growth will depend on how much of that money is actually spent at home.

Greg Bonnell: So we have a Bank of Canada that got a little more accommodative for us this year. We have inflation-- there's a bit of stickiness here and there, but it's sort of in the ballpark. What can we expect for the Bank of Canada next year?

Andrew Kelvin: So the fact that the Bank of Canada is in accommodative territory is one of the things that does help a little bit growth next year. It's not a huge needle mover, but 50 basis points on the overnight rate does have a positive impact on growth. From the Bank of Canada's perspective, they think rates are about right if their forecast holds. Their forecast is actually quite similar to our forecast for 2026.

So, it follows that if we are roughly correct on growth and inflation, and we see inflation coming lower through the year-- core measures of inflation, I should say-- if we are roughly correct on growth and inflation, it follows that the Bank of Canada is probably done here for 2026, at least.

Greg Bonnell: When they're not done anymore. So for 2026, if they're done, if everything goes to plan, when we come out of that-- we've still got a long way to go-- are we talking about a bank that will start taking away accommodation if this economy can start growing?

Andrew Kelvin: The economy starts growing, and we can take some of the slack in the economy. So we would expect to see a much lower unemployment rate in this sort of world. But if the economy grows, as we project it will, we would see the unemployment rate fall.

We would see slack reduced in the economy, and we would expect the bank to lift rates-- we think starting in early 2027-- because we are at an accommodative level of interest rates. Textbook says that if you get back to an at-trend growth rate and you have no slack in the economy, interest rates should be around their neutral level, which the Bank of Canada sees at 2.75.

Now, in terms of where the shocks are-- because I can have my empirically grounded, mean-reverting model of how the economy is going to behave, but then we can have things come along, like trade wars, which models tend not to incorporate. So, there's a reasonable degree of symmetry around that forecast-- if we are incorrect in the idea that trade impacts will be lesser this year, if we actually see the same drag on growth from trade in '26 as we did in '25-- then we're probably not seeing hikes in '27.

And in fact, we could see additional cuts next year. There's definitely worlds where they can ease. But I think you look at the risks as skewed a little bit more towards 2027 hikes than towards 2026 cuts.

Greg Bonnell: Is that the biggest risk for 2026 in terms of that forecast thinking, what the economy is going to produce, what the Bank of Canada will do? Is it those trade negotiations that are coming back up for USMCA CUSMA?

Andrew Kelvin: I think that's the biggest one, in part because there's a wide variety of outcomes. My thesis has been for a long time that, (a) the US government will not take actions that raise domestic energy prices, which should allow our energy sector to continue exporting to the United States without tariff impediments.

And (b), the fact that USMCA exemptions are in the current trading stance of the United States, it suggests to me that somebody in the US sees some value in keeping these USMCA exemptions. So I expect those exemptions to persist through 2026 until we actually sign the new trade deal, which, again, we do anticipate just ahead of the deadline on July 1st.

I'm making a lot of assumptions here. If there's a world where there is as a negotiating tactic, or there is some sort of flare-up in relations between the two countries-- we have seen that intermittently in recent months that there will be points of friction that seemingly come out of nowhere. If it turns out that the trading terms between Canada and the US worsen, my forecast is probably too optimistic.

The other critical assumption, though, is that the government is able to get the money out the door in an effective way in the timelines they anticipate. It's very easy to put these sort of numbers down on paper, saying we intend to spend x billion dollars by this date. Being able to do that effectively in a way that boosts the economy is actually a difficult task.

I have no reason to doubt that the government is going to be able to do it, but it is an assumption that the money gets out the door in a way that boosts growth. That assumption could prove to be-- could be that we are too conservative in our estimates, it could be we're too optimistic. There's symmetry around that as well.

Greg Bonnell: Alright. We put all of this together. What could it mean for the bond market?

Andrew Kelvin: So the big question for the bond market almost has nothing to do with Canada, to be perfectly honest with you. There was a reduction in government of Canada borrowing associated with that budget, which caught some people by surprise. I think, really, what that comes down to is the government has fewer bonds maturing next year, so they need to issue fewer bonds to refinance that maturing debt. It's still a big borrowing program, nearly $300 billion. The reductions we saw were more in shorter-duration bonds than long-duration bonds.

So the duration hitting the market in Canada-- once you factor in Canada mortgage bonds, once you factor in provincial bonds and all those sorts of things-- we see the bond market supply is roughly stable in a world where Canada is pretty rich to the US. So if you were to just hold US rates constant here, my bias would be to see Canadian yields drift a little bit higher, particularly because we also have seen the market maybe being a little bit too cautious in pricing and Bank of Canada normalization in 2027-2028.

The thing is, we don't think the US bond market is going to be stable here. I think one of the really interesting lessons of the last 12-18 months has been that the world's actually able to absorb this degree of government supply. We expect stable US issuance next year in duration terms.

Net coupon issuance-- so, issuance net of Fed buying-- is actually expected to fall next year in the United States. We see European issuance essentially stable as well. So we're in a world where global bond markets were able to deal with the amount of supply we saw last year. We think we'll see about the same amount of supply this year.

So we think that's not really an issue in terms of markets being able to deal with these large deficits in the US, Europe, Japan, what have you. At the same time, the Fed keeps cutting. And as the Fed cuts, we think US yields will move lower broadly. There are strong correlations between US rates and Canadian rates.

So we actually think lower US yields will put downward pressure on Canadian yields. We actually expect modestly lower yields in Canada at the 10-year point. We see Canadian 10-year bonds finishing next year around 3%.

Original Post

data.title: Jobs Update: The Gig Economy May Mask A Growing Unemployment Problem
data.description:
Myriad data points in recent months suggest a softening labor market, but even the more dour stats may actually be understating the situation in some cases. Crucially, Goldman Sachs released a report showing that laid-off workers may be increasingly turning to gig work to get by. Roughly 20% of laid-off workers turned to gig work, according to the report. However, earnings were a mere 50 to 65% of what they were previously earning. This comes as continuing unemployment claims have steadily ticked upward, suggesting that laid-off workers are taking longer to find new employment opportunities

That said, the September jobs report, delayed due to the government shutdown and only now released, showed that 119,000 jobs were added. We’re not likely to get the October report, which is crucial, as some indicators hinted at trouble last month. Also, the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4%. All in all, economists had expected only 50,000 jobs to be added but also anticipated that the unemployment rate would hold steady. Part of the uptick in the unemployment rate would have been due to increased job loss in prior months. Jobs data in July was revised to 72,000 jobs added, from the previously projected 79,000 jobs added. Previously, it was thought that 22,000 jobs were added in August, but the revised data showed 4,000 jobs lost.

The next jobs data release is scheduled for December 16th, 11 days later than initially projected, with the delay being due to the government shutdown. The big question now is whether the Federal Reserve will move to cut interest rates. The stronger-than-expected jobs report may encourage the Fed to pause rate cuts, especially with the most recent inflation reading showing a 3% increase in September, which is a full percentage point above the Fed’s target of 2.

The biggest question here is probably the missing jobs report from October. Further, downward revisions of past job reports have become increasingly common, perhaps hinting at a disconnection between how the data is collected and the reality on the ground. This history of revisions may encourage Federal Chair Jerome Powell and other moderates on the FOMC to lean towards continued rate cuts, especially given that Powell has already come under accusations of acting too slowly.

Mr. Powell would also be wise to take a look at the recent Goldman Sachs report, which I’ll take a closer look at below. Tracking employment levels has never been easy, but the rise of the app-fueled gig economy has only made things murkier.

How The Gig Economy May Be Obscuring Unemployment

The gig economy isn’t really all that new. People have long been picking up odd jobs to get by. However, in recent years, the increased usage of apps and websites to connect gig workers with gig customers has supercharged the industry. Uber and Lyft, among other apps, for example, made it easy for pretty much anyone with a somewhat recent model car to become a taxi driver either full-time or on the side. Gig work offers flexibility and affords people the opportunity to work on their own schedule and, to some extent, be their own boss (a dream for many).

Analysts at Goldman Sachs took a deep dive into the gig economy and how it might impact unemployment rates. Crucially, the completed analysis found that between 5 and 15% of Americans participate in the gig economy. The wide disparity here hints at how difficult it is to track gig work. Yet researchers have found that over the past few years, gig work has likely accelerated to a 5 to 8% annualized growth in gig engagement.

The study further found that around 20% of people who lost their jobs, saw hours reduced, or otherwise had to take a pay cut turned to gig work to sustain income. This flexibility, at first glance, might be encouraging. However, the study also found that people typically only made between 50 and 65% per hour compared to their prior earnings, and many jobs average $15 or less per hour. Further, on average, people work just 14 hours completing gigs per week. Low pay and low hours suggest that gig work is not a good replacement for traditional jobs.

Further, Goldman Sachs’ researchers also found that in cities where job growth has been slowing the quickest, gig work engagement has been accelerating. So while gig work growth overall has been modest in recent years, it’s crucial to take the historically strong American economy into context and also to now note the softening economy.

Gig workers likely aren’t captured in full in jobs reports because the data is collected based on estimates from businesses and establishments. Household surveys may better capture gig workers as they focus on individuals and families, although the general confusion around gig work and underreporting could skew statistics. Many gig workers are likely marginally attached to the economy and looking for full-time employment. The U6 unemployment rate tracks marginally attached individuals, and this alternative unemployment rate, which captures more people than the often cited U-3 unemployment rate, has generally ticked upward in recent months. The U-6 (bottom chart) shows faster overall growth in recent months, although it did cool a bit in September, whereas the U-3 rate continued to increase.

Keep in mind that the economy is still technically strong, even if concerns are increasing. Should we enter a recession, it’s quite likely that far more people will turn to gig work to get by. At this stage, October is shaping up to be a crucial month. If October’s labor market showed renewed strength, it could suggest that the recent economic turbulence was temporary and that rate cuts are already paying off. Unfortunately, we won’t be getting October data from the Federal government. ADP job reports found that private employers added 42,000 jobs in October, while a separate Goldman Sachs report found that the economy overall probably lost 50,000 jobs (in Oct.).

Key Takeaway: Concerns Remain Real But Labor Market Chugs Along

The gig economy is likely papering over issues with the American job market. While it’s a good thing that people can use alternative employment opportunities to gain income, gig work is less secure and typically lower paid per hour than traditional employment. Further, should a recession set in and unemployment rise dramatically, rates paid to gig workers will likely plummet. The September jobs report showed stable, positive growth, but the October data, at this point, is probably more crucial, and we’ll likely not be getting the data soon, if ever at all.

data.title: Moving From TC Energy To The Next Higher Yield And Dividend Growth Bet: ONEOK
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

Earlier this year, I shared that I was writing covered calls on my TC Energy (TRP) position after a very successful run. Since then, shares have rallied quite sharply higher, a bit to my surprise, but nothing that I'm going to get too overly worked up about.

Given the expectation that shares are looking to be called away at the $52.50 strike, I've made a move to turn to writing puts and initiate a position on ONEOK (OKE). This is a smaller midstream company, but one that has seen its shares heading in a different direction compared to TRP.

With that, here is why I made my latest move of buying OKE shares, but also writing some puts as well. Here is exactly what I did:

The Trades:

  • First: Adding ONEOK (OKE) @ $68.91 for the Satellite Income Builder Portfolio
  • Second: Sell to Open puts on ONEOK (OKE) January 16, 2026 - $65 strike collected $1.69

Additionally, this article will highlight why comfortable with TRP being highly likely to be called away.

Debt Levels

On the debt front, we can see that TRP carries a higher relative level of debt compared to OKE.

Both of these are currently above the 3-4x net debt to adjusted EBITDA levels that midstream companies have been targeting these days. They also both still trade above their own target levels.

For TRP, they are generally targeting a relatively higher amount of debt carry, in general, as remarked in their latest earnings call.

Driven by exceptional project execution and capital optimization, we now expect 2025 net capital expenditures to be at the low end of our $5.5 billion to $6 billion range. When you combine that with our expected growth in comparable EBITDA, we have clear line of sight to achieving our long-term target of 4.75x debt-to-EBITDA, ensuring continued financial flexibility for future growth.

That also includes a relatively higher level when compared to OKE's longer-term target range. They had this remark in their latest earnings call when questioned on capital allocation overall, and the $45 million in buybacks done in the quarter.

Well, as we've said in the past, as we get closer to a clear path to our debt-to-EBITDA target of 3.5x, it's going to free up our flexibility to add some stock buybacks to the equation. We continue to be on track with where we think we need to get to from a debt-to-EBITDA standpoint. And with that visibility, we're starting to feel a little bit more flexible in our asset allocation, saw the opportunity to buy back some stock there in the third quarter and did a modest amount. We also saw a pretty nice opportunity on the bond side and executed on that as well.

There is nothing wrong with utilizing debt in an effort to grow. In particular, midstreams is a very capital-intensive industry with massive projects that have significant costs. These projects then generally have pretty steady, predictable cash flows that help to ease anxiety about repayments—thus allowing higher levels of debt that can be appropriate. That said, a higher relative debt level can leave a company more vulnerable, as a balance sheet can remain more constrained.

Growth Expectations

As expected, both are continuing to expect EBITDA growth in the future; after all, that's what all the capex spend and debt accumulation in the past is supposed to serve over the medium and longer term. Growing EBITDA naturally means a lower debt/EBITDA ratio, even if one excludes debt paydowns.

On this front, TRP is looking like it'll actually see a slight dip in EBITDA for 2025, resuming an upward trending. Analysts expected average EBITDA growth of ~5% from 2026 through 2029.

This is not too far out of line with what the management team is guiding for at TRP, either.

For OKE, we've seen a significantly more rapid EBITDA growth in recent years, and over the longer term, analysts estimate a more tepid growth resuming.

OKE's EBITDA jump has been material, thanks to larger acquisitions that helped to generate a substantial amount of earnings growth for the company.

However, it is these acquisitions that appear to have put a damper on the share price for OKE investors, giving some uncertainty about whether the company can pull off its expectations.

For 2025, analysts are estimating results come in on the lower end of management's guidance; if there is any surprise to the upside, that could send shares of OKE flying higher. Of course, the opposite is true; if they don't manage to meet even lower relative analyst estimates, shares could continue to sink.

Valuation Favors OKE

My ultimate switch from being bullish on TRP and toward OKE instead is based on valuation. That doesn't always work out in the short or even medium term, but generally over the longer term, this fundamental focus can pay off.

For TRP, we are trading over the longer-term average EV/EBITDA for the name.

OKE, thanks to the slumping share price and what I believe is uncertainty about the larger acquisitions paying off, is trading well below its longer-term average.

However, it is worth noting that TRP does often trade at a higher multiple relative to OKE—meaning investors tend to value TRP more highly. For me, it is relative to where they are trading against themselves ultimately.

One other way that investors can place a valuation on dividend payers such as these is through the dividend yield. As the share price has risen for TRP, despite the dividend growth, the shares are currently trading at a lower dividend yield relative to their historical level.

For U.S. investors, dealing with the USD/CAD conversion has meant more noise in terms of growth in the dividend. However, the company is in its 25th year of consecutive dividend boosts.

OKE has seen a lot of noise based on what looks like a much more volatile price chart, with a rapid decline in 2020 before bouncing back. The latest yield is only slightly above its longer-term average.

They don't have many years of consecutive increases like TRP, because during Covid, they held their quarterly payout flat at $0.9350 for 12 quarters in a row—since then, they have once again resumed the increases. They can also boast of 27 years of paying dividends with no cuts during this time, just freezing at some levels when it is warranted.

For what it's worth, when we originally bought into TRP, the dividend yield was pushing around 8.2%. So the OKE yield isn't as "high yield" as our originally TRP pick up, but at ~6%, I still consider it a relatively high yield regardless.

Conclusion

TRP has treated us very well. We originally picked up our position in July 2023:

The Trade:

  • Buying TC Energy Corp (TRP) @ $34.89 for the Satellite Income Builder Portfolio

The price is rising today quite sharply, but shares are quite beaten down as discussed in the commentary.

Selling the position at what looks like $52.50 via covered calls while I believe shares are currently relatively richly priced makes sense to me. That's a nearly 51% return on share price alone, while collecting $6.199 in dividends for USD investors. Then, since we switched to writing calls, the premiums along the way tally up to another $3.60.

Ticker Expiration Date Upper Strike Current Price Move Until Strike Type Sold Date Initiated Premium Collected Date Closed Closing Cost Gain/Loss
TRP 11/21/2025 $52.50 $54.21 -3.15% Calls 08/11/2025 $0.87 TBD TBD TBD
TRP 08/15/2025 $50.00 Rolled - Calls 05/13/2025 $1.22 08/11/2025 $0.32 $0.90
TRP 05/16/2025 $50.00 Rolled - Calls 03/18/2025 $0.73 05/13/2025 $0.07 $0.66
TRP 02/21/2025 $50.00 Expired - Calls 12/02/2024 $1.17 Expired - $1.17

Even further, TRP remains a part of my portfolio in a small way...through the South Bow Corporation (SOBO) spin-off. That brought my average cost basis to adjust to $31.80 from the closer to $35 level at the time of the trade alert posting.

With that, it will remain to be seen if OKE can deliver such a success, but at these better relative and more tempting valuations and attractive yield, I'm willing to take that risk.

data.title: American Century Mid Cap Value Fund Q3 2025 Contributors/Detractors And Notable Trades
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the American Century Mid Cap Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

Key Contributors

Teradyne (TER). Shares of Teradyne advanced sharply as investors cheered the company’s robust earnings and outlook for Teradyne’s artificial intelligence compute testing business.

Baker Hughes (BKR). This oil field services company rallied after reporting earnings that beat analysts’ expectations. We believe there have been signs that indicate increased capital spending on oil and gas infrastructure globally, an area in which Baker Hughes is considered a leader.

Charter Communications (CHTR). Not owning this mass media and telecommunications company benefited performance. Shares of Charter Communications fell after posting disappointing earnings amid worse-than-expected broadband losses.

Key Detractors

Kenvue (KVUE). Shares of this personal care products company underperformed due to negative press associated with the Trump administration’s claims about potential ties between the usage of Tylenol during pregnancy and autism. Though disappointing, we believe the issue is manageable for Kenvue.

Robinhood Markets (HOOD). A lack of exposure to this electronic trading platform detracted from performance. Robinhood’s shares have advanced as it announced new offerings through partners, including banking and sports betting.

Henry Schein (HSIC). Shares of this dental products distributor declined as promotional activity to attract new customers in its online channel pressured margins.

Notable Trades

PACCAR (PCAR). We initiated a position in this commercial truck manufacturer, which we believe has become attractively valued relative to its industrial peers as expectations for truck demand have continued to be revised lower.

Publicis Groupe (OTCQX:PUBGY). We initiated a position in this France-based advertising and public relations company because we believe this high-quality business is well positioned to gain new client accounts.

Centene (CNC). We exited our position in this managed care organization and reallocated proceeds to other positions that we believed offered more attractive risk/reward opportunities.

The Interpublic Group of Cos (IPG). We exited our position in this advertising agency company as it is set to be acquired by Omnicom Group later this year, following regulatory approvals. We reallocated funds to other investments that we believed had more attractive risk/reward profiles.

You should consider the fund’s investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses carefully before you invest. The fund’s prospectus or summary prospectus, which can be obtained at American Century Investments® | Managing Money, Making an Impact, contains this and other information about the fund, and should be read carefully before investing.

The opinions expressed are those of the portfolio investment team and are no guarantee of the future performance of any American Century Investments portfolio. Statements regarding specific holdings represent personal views and compensation has not been received in connection with such views. This information is for an educational purpose only and is not intended to serve as investment advice.

The information is not intended as a personalized recommendation or fiduciary advice and should not be relied upon for investment, accounting, legal or tax advice.

The fund is generally closed to new investors other than those who (i) invest directly with American Century (where American Century is listed as the dealer of record); (ii) invest through certain financial intermediaries selected by American Century; or (iii) otherwise qualify for an exemption under American Century’s closed fund policy.

Historically, small- and/or mid-cap stocks have been more volatile than the stocks of larger, more established companies. Smaller companies may have limited resources, product lines and markets, and their securities may trade less frequently and in more limited volumes than those of larger companies.

The Russell Midcap® Index measures the performance of the 800 smallest companies in the Russell 1000® Index. The Russell Midcap® Value Index measures the performance of those Russell Midcap® companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth values. The stocks are also members of the Russell 1000® Value Index. Created by Frank Russell Company, indices are not investment products available for purchase.

American Century Investments Services, Inc., Distributor ©2025

American Century Proprietary Holdings, Inc. All rights reserved.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: September Jobs Report Boosts Unemployment, But Rate Cut Still Likely
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, the Bureau of Labor Statistics released the labor market report for September. The report, delayed by approximately 6 weeks due to the government shutdown, showed the labor market created a better-than-expected 119,000 jobs in the month of September. Despite the better-than-expected jobs number, the unemployment rate unexpectedly ticked up 0.1% to 4.4%. While the Fed may be showing some anxiety towards further rate cuts (and I’m in support of holding), I believe the Central Bank will follow through with an additional rate cut in December.

Leading Indicators To September Jobs Report

While we had a blackout of data for some time prior to the government shutdown, the Department of Labor released three weeks of jobless claims data that gave us a preview into the condition of the labor market in September. This data showed that initial jobless claims had reversed dramatically from a spike and were sitting at nearly the lowest levels of the year. Continuing claims, which incorporate unemployed people rolling over from the previous week, gradually eased down throughout the month but remained above the 1.9 million level that was suddenly reached in the spring. Overall, the jobless claims supported growth in hiring within the economy.

While the JOLTS data is a little older (dating back to August), it showed some of why August’s numbers were revised down to negative. During the month of August, hiring in the economy slowed dramatically. While separations in the labor market also slowed, hiring had fallen to either in tandem or below separations for three consecutive months. This would indicate low to negative job growth and a higher unemployment rate, and explain why the Fed has become so nervous about the labor market.

What Does The Federal Reserve Do From Here?

While there’s considerable chatter surrounding the possibility that the Federal Reserve will hold in December, and it’s a position I agree with, I don’t think it’s going to happen. The Federal Reserve has made it clear that the current policy regime is centered around protecting the labor market portion of its dual mandate. In fact, while the unemployment rate is rising, it is moving towards the Fed’s policy estimate of 4.5% for year-end, which also coincides with the estimate of another 25-basis point cut.

Possibly, the most compelling data point for another Fed cut is looking at job creation over the past few years. On a trailing twelve-month basis, the economy has created just over 1.3 million jobs. While that may sound robust, it is the smallest year-over-year gain in the past-pandemic era and signals that the labor market may still be on a weakening trajectory.

Looking Ahead: Keep An Eye On Continuing Claims & The Labor Force

In addition to September labor market data, we got our first look at the current state of the labor market this morning with the release of the weekly jobless claims. While the initial claims number was tame, the continuing claims surged to 1.97 million. We won’t know for a few weeks how much of this was related to the government shutdown, but higher continuing claims give me pause that the progress made in job creation in September will be replicated in October and November. We should get a couple more weeks of jobless claims data before the Federal Reserve’s next meeting.

While job creation will play a big role in the future unemployment rate, the number of people entering and leaving the workforce will also have an effect. The reason that the number of jobs created outperformed expectations and the unemployment rate underperformed in September was because people not in the labor force re-entered, which applied upward pressure to the unemployment rate. If this continues, the unemployment rate could jump over the Federal Reserve’s 2025 target.

Conclusion

For controlling inflation, a pause in rate paths is likely warranted by the Federal Reserve. But the importance of the labor market over price stability in the near term is going to likely lead to another cut. As it currently stands, the Fed meeting is scheduled to occur the week before the next monthly labor market reports. Unless the Fed has early access, they may want to consider delaying their next meeting.

data.title: HEQ: Discount Remains Wide For This Diversified Fund
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

John Hancock Diversified Income Fund (HEQ) changed its name from John Hancock Hedged Equity & Income Fund late last year. Along with the name change came an investment policy change as well, which we outlined in our prior update. The main shift was that instead of it being more specifically an equity-focused fund that included hedging through the use of options, it shifted to include more fixed-income exposure while dropping the options.

However, when we last gave the fund a look, we also noted that the fund did not employ any options during the last annual report anyway. Instead, they appeared to implement futures and forward foreign currency contracts as hedging vehicles.

One thing that has remained the same for this fund is that the fund's discount has remained quite wide. In fact, it has hardly budged since our last update and sits at a double-digit-wide discount. This keeps it remaining at a historically wide discount, but investors have fairly consistently kept it at this wider level since the 2023 distribution cut. That makes it an interesting candidate to consider for an investor on the hunt for a flexible hybrid fund, but it's not necessarily a screaming buy, given this could be the new 'normal' trading discount level.

HEQ Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.54.
  • Discount/Premium: -11.26%.
  • Distribution Yield: 9.33%.
  • Expense Ratio: 1.26%.
  • Leverage: N/A.
  • Managed Assets: $146.8 million.
  • Structure: Perpetual.

HEQ's investment objective is to "seek total return consisting of both income and capital gains." The investment objective did not change with the late 2024 changes of the fund.

In an attempt to achieve the objectives, the fund:

Under normal circumstances, the fund will invest at least 25% of its net assets (assets plus borrowings for investment purposes) in equity and equity-related securities and at least 25% in fixed-income securities and fixed-income related instruments. Equity and equity-related securities include common stock, preferred stock, depositary receipts (including American Depositary Receipts and Global Depositary Receipts), index-related securities (including exchange traded funds ("ETFs"), options on equity securities and equity indexes, real estate investment structures (including real estate investment trusts ("REITs")), convertible securities, private placements, convertible preferred stock, rights, warrants, derivatives linked to equity securities or indexes and other similar equity equivalents. Fixed-income related securities may include, but are not limited to, instruments such as: non-investment grade ("high yield" or "junk bond") instruments, swaps, futures, forwards, options, mortgages, and bank loans.

Performance—Discount Remains Wide

Since our prior update, the fund has delivered positive total returns. Though it fell short of the S&P 500 Index during this time, given the fixed-income exposure and continued hedging through derivatives like futures, that isn't all that unexpected. Fixed-income exposure is, in particular, likely one of the main drags in performance due to the substantial gain we've seen in the broader equity market.

Instead, for performance, the fund provides its own relative benchmarks. That includes a Global Aggressive Allocation category, MSCI ACWI, as well as its "Blended Benchmark." Within the blended benchmark is "60% MSCI All Country World Index ("ACWI") and 40% Intercontinental Exchange ("ICE") Bank of America Global High Yield Constrained Index." That helps represent both its global reach and also a heavier allocation to fixed-income exposure these days.

In general, the fund has regularly underperformed all of these relevant benchmarks that it includes. The only time it outperformed was on a net asset value basis with the 5-year mark against its blended index.

That said, we should give a bit more scrutiny to even this before getting too excited. This is because the blended index is heavier toward fixed income, and the 5-year period reflects when fixed income saw significant losses due to aggressive Fed rate hikes. The investment policy to include more fixed income for HEQ started in late 2024, meaning that it was still a heavier equity fund during most of that period.

On the other hand, for the counterargument, one could present the fact that the fund was hedged—and still is incorporating future and forward foreign currency contracts, even now. Hedges come with a cost, and that can often weigh on longer-term performance when the general trend is heading higher for equities.

So while the total returns may not be too impressive relative to their benchmarks, they also aren't necessarily awful when looking at them in isolation. Still, picking up this fund at a wide discount would appear to be the more attractive approach to help potentially juice the upside returns, should the discount ever narrow materially.

The fund is currently trading below its longer-term average level. In fact, at one time, this fund traded at premium levels, but it has been quite some time since.

The last 3-year average discount comes in at around 10%, which makes it less appealing if investors are hoping for a quick narrowing of the discount. With no activist investors in this fund, there doesn't appear to be a clear catalyst for the discount to close soon. There is always the chance of something, but without pressure, managers tend not to have much incentive. There is always a chance that the distribution could be lifted materially, which often generates investor attention—or additionally, announcing a merger with another one of their CEFs that trades at a narrower discount or even a premium is a possibility.

The latter seems like a more viable solution, but they don't necessarily have a similar fund like HEQ to easily fold it into. Instead, it would have to change the focus to more of a preferred fund, such as John Hancock Preferred Income Fund (HPI). With its ~6% premium, that would make it an interesting choice and one that investors would have good reason to vote to approve.

Distribution—Bumped Due To Substantial Discount

The fund currently pays out a quarterly distribution, with a NAV rate of 8.28%, but the market rate is coming in at 9.33%. The bump in the market rate is thanks to the fund trading at a meaningful discount.

Over the long term, the fund has seen quite a bit of a steady trend of NAV declines, which would suggest that the fund has not been able to cover its distribution. For funds with heavy equity allocations, watching the NAV over time is the best and quickest gauge of coverage. This is because the fund will require primarily capital gains to provide coverage for the payout, rather than the income received from the underlying holdings in the form of dividends and interest payments.

For HEQ, the last semi-annual report indicated that net investment income coverage came in at 68%. That's actually on the higher end for a fund that is tilted toward equity, but that makes sense given a shift toward more fixed-income exposure over the last year. In 2024, the fund's NII coverage came in at 52%, for some context.

Of course, even with NAV and share price declines, we saw above that total returns can still be partially respectable, and it doesn't always mean that the fund is producing losses, as the distributions offset the declines.

For tax purposes, the fund's distribution has been characterized mostly as ordinary income in the 2024 year; however, drilling it down further, the fund's $1 paid was $0.457588 in qualified dividends and $0.226004 in non-dividend distribution (aka return of capital). That leaves a relatively smaller portion of $0.316408 as being subject to ordinary income tax rates.

HEQ's Portfolio

During our last update, the fund was still tilted quite heavily toward equity positions at around 70% of the fund. This has come down to around 63% of the fund now, according to CEFConnect, as of September 30, 2025. The fund also lists futures and forward foreign currency contracts as of its latest semi-annual report. Additionally, the fund also has some credit default swaps, but the exposure there is quite minimal. That said, the semi-annual report is for the six months ended June 30, 2025, so it's a bit more dated.

Shifting to the fund's website, they outline the broader asset composition of the portfolio as of October 31, 2025. On that front, we've seen a slight shift to a higher allocation of collateralized mortgage obligations from the ~9% level previously—ABS exposure also shifted a bit higher, but not materially. This would indicate some shift to fixed-income securities; however, with the sector allocation, it doesn't define more specifically what is allocated to equity versus fixed income.

With that said, we can see just how diversified the fund is, as the name would imply. This is then followed through not only on the types of securities the fund holds, but also reflected in the over 550 different positions the fund carries as well. Worth also noting is that the fund carries a heavier exposure to financials compared to the heavier tilt toward technology, which has comprised larger swaths of the S&P 500 Index these days.

Moving to the top ten holdings also further solidifies its 'diversified' name, given that the largest allocation is to JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) at over a 1% allocation, but all the other holdings are sub-1% level—which actually drops quite rapidly with the tenth spot, Philip Morris International Inc. (PM), coming in at a 0.63% weight.

Conclusion

HEQ trades at a deep discount to its net asset value, but this appears to be more of the new level of the fund. They shifted the fund's investment policy and name around late last year. However, that hasn't seemed to make investors any more interested in this relatively smaller fund. While it is an interesting name, and I'm quite fond of John Hancock, the fund sponsor, this is another one of those funds that doesn't seem to have a clear catalyst for closing the discount. Without activist exposure in the fund, the management team doesn't have a lot of incentive to make a meaningful effort to reduce the discount.

data.title: 4 Reasons The Odds Of A Santa Claus Rally Just Went Up Big
data.description:
I have been bearish recently, and the market has taken a hit over the past few days; however, I am getting a bit more positive (at least in the short term) that the chances of a year-end rally are getting much more likely. I have been calling for a pullback, and now that the market is down about 5% from recent highs, there has been a small pullback, which makes the setup going into the end of the year more positive. There are also a few other factors that could make a year-end rally more likely, and one of them is the potential for a rate cut in December. I also believe that President Trump is seeing that consumers are feeling pinched by high prices and that is a motivating factor for him to "juice" the economy before the midterm elections.

We all saw a socialist Democrat win the mayor's race in New York City by running on a platform that promises many free things and even a government-run grocery store, and Democrats won in other key races. I believe this raises the stakes for the Trump Administration to create some policies that could ease the pressure on consumers. The recent economic data that shows deterioration in the job market and declining consumer sentiment is likely putting pressure on the Fed to cut rates, and that pressure probably just increased a bit with the stock market declining by about 5%. The last thing the Fed potentially wants is to see the economy continue to weaken and have the stock market melt down in December, which could further crimp consumer sentiment and the levels of shopping during the big holiday shopping season.

Reason #1: A Key Earnings Report Was A Hurdle That Was Just Cleared

With tech stocks not trading well in recent days, I believed investors needed to get past Nvidia's (NVDA) earnings report and see that this company beat earnings, which is exactly what it did. Clearing this Nvidia earnings hurdle allows the market to move forward, and with the stock up after the earnings report, it is one less major event for investors to worry about. The strong earnings and guidance from Nvidia should be enough to get the tech stock rally back on track.

Reason #2: I Believe A Fed Rate Cut In December Is Coming

There has been a clear softening trend in the jobs data in recent months—whether you look at the government data that was provided before the shutdown or at the more recent private payrolls data, it shows rapid deterioration. Challenger, Gray & Christmas recently announced that 153,074 jobs were cut in October. This represents a 183% increase from September. The number of layoff announcements in 2025 has made it the worst year for announced layoffs since 2009, and that is something that the Fed has to take seriously. Aside from the layoffs that are due to DOGE job cuts in the government sector and some layoffs among many small businesses, potentially due to tariffs, there has also been a new wave of layoffs that are being blamed on AI and automation, which seem to be gaining steam.

A recent report from Bernie Sanders suggests that 100 million jobs could be replaced by AI in the next ten years. This potentially represents about 10 million lost jobs each year on average for the next ten years. This could be a wave of layoffs that we have not seen before, and it is something that the Fed has to start building into its forecasts and policies because if they don't, unemployment could surge. When there is a high level of unemployment, it can put pressure on wages and bonuses paid to those that still have jobs, and that could limit wage growth and consumer spending. Many mega-cap tech companies have started to lay off workers that are being replaced by AI, and some companies are just not hiring new college graduates because jobs that could have gone to a college graduate are now being replaced by AI in some cases. This seems to be creating a new drag on the economy for even middle-class and wealthy families who are being forced to support their adult children who are not finding the first job they were expecting after graduation.

It is clear that the Fed is going to be challenged in getting inflation to drop, which is currently running at about 3%, down to its stated target of 2%. Since the Fed is not going to get rid of inflation anytime soon, they might as well try to win the battle against further job losses by cutting rates as an "insurance policy" against the weakening job market and a potential recession. I don't believe a 1/4 point rate cut will cause a surge in inflation, and the job cuts and lack of hiring we are seeing are very disinflationary and that should appease any of the more hawkish members of the Fed.

I believe the Federal Reserve is not just looking at the jobs market, because consumer sentiment is an important metric when considering the health of the economy, and consumer sentiment recently hit an all-time low—and loan delinquencies are rising. This is worrisome since consumer spending represents about 2/3rds of the U.S. economy. This weak consumer sentiment and increasing delinquencies imply inflationary pressures are likely to be easing, and that should give the Fed room to cut rates in December.

I want to add that even if the Fed does not cut rates in December, it makes it likely that the rate cut will just get delayed slightly, since they have another meeting in January during which they can cut rates. I believe this means that if there is no December rate cut, it won't matter too much, as that just makes a January rate cut more likely, and the Fed is likely to communicate this in December, which would reassure the market and allow for a Santa Claus rally.

Reason #3: President Trump Might Take Action To Boost The Consumer And The Chances For A Year-End Rally

I think the chances of a year-end rally are increasing because the stock market has just taken out a lot of the froth and reset valuations for many stocks, and I also think a Fed rate cut in December is likely to happen and help create a year-end rally. However, I believe that President Trump is also now floating a few ideas that the market could start to focus on that might give investors hope for 2026, and this could also ignite the often hoped-for Santa Claus rally.

As I recently wrote about in this article, President Trump seems to be seeing the weakness in the economy and the pressure facing many consumers, as he recently proposed a $2,000 tariff dividend. In this article I also mentioned that tax refunds are poised to be larger in 2026, and this could boost consumer confidence and spending early next year. Investors could look at these factors as being a source of relief and a reason to be bullish towards what is seasonally a strong time for stocks at the end of each year. President Trump also just recently announced a reduction in tariffs on many major food items in order to take the pressure off of consumers. This could help reduce food inflation concerns that the Fed might have. As I wrote in this article, President Trump recently proposed a 50-year mortgage in order to make housing more affordable. I view this as more of a gimmick than as a real solution, but it does show that President Trump is focusing on boosting the consumer and the economy.

Reason #4: The Stock Market May Have Found Support At This Key Level, Which Sets Up Nicely For A Year-End Rally

The S&P 500 Index (SPY) has recently declined about 5% from the recent highs, and this 5% decline is a level where some investors like to buy the dip. But the other factor is that the recent selloff has put this index back down to around the 50-day moving average, a key level that is shown by the blue trend line on the chart below. This could be an area of support that sets the market up for a year-end rally.

Here's My Year-End Buying Strategy

Amazon (AMZN) shares surged to around $255 after the company reported Q3 earnings, but the stock has lost almost all the gains and just traded for about $220. This appears to be an ideal year-end buying opportunity, and I bought more shares at around $220 and will add more around this level. I am also focusing on beaten-down stocks that are probably being impacted by tax-loss selling at this time of year. This means I am buying stocks like Whirlpool (WHR), Stanley Black & Decker (SWK), Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG), Deckers (DECK), and others that fit this profile. Buying stocks that are being impacted by tax-loss selling can lead to gains in the coming weeks when this tax-loss selling fades.

Where I Could Be Wrong

There are still hawkish comments coming from some Fed members, and if the Fed does not cut rates in December, this could cause a fresh wave of selling that takes the market lower. Some of President Trump's proposals may not happen or may not be enough to move the needle in terms of the economy and the stock market. If more negative economic data is released, it could put more pressure on stock market valuations, even if the Fed cuts rates.

In Summary

I believe President Trump and the Fed are seeing the weakness in the job market and consumer sentiment, and I believe both will respond to this soon, which could fuel a seasonal year-end rally. I am still bearish on the economy and the stock market overall, but I see a potential tradeable rally opportunity at year-end. This is because the market has recently experienced a pullback and also because I believe the Fed could give the market a rate cut that it needs to boost stocks and the economy during the all-important month of December. For this reason, I would put a hold rating on the S&P 500 Index and consider selling it into strength if we see a year-end rally.

data.title: Ginkgo Bioworks: Scaling Science, Not Revenue
data.description:
Ginkgo Bioworks Holdings, Inc.'s (DNA) business remained steady in the third quarter, and guidance suggests this situation will persist until at least the end of the year. While cost-cutting efforts are helping to reduce Ginkgo's cash burn, the company needs to return to growth in order to transition to profitability. There is little reason to be optimistic on this front, as Ginkgo's Tools and Services businesses are well over 12 months old and yet to create a growth resurgence. Ginkgo's Biosecurity business also continues to tread water. Some of this is likely due to macro conditions, but it is hard not to feel that Ginkgo doesn't have its business model figured out and that its value proposition isn't resonating with customers. This is an issue I first raised in early 2022, and Ginkgo's share price is now down over 90% since then.

I previously highlighted how it would be difficult for Ginkgo Bioworks to transition to profitability due to its inability to generate growth. Ginkgo still has a substantial cash balance, but its latest equity raise points towards management caution regarding its financial strength. Even if Ginkgo doesn't need to continue raising capital, investors face the threat of ruinous dilution, due in large part to excessive SBC. As a result, I remain neutral to negative on Ginkgo's prospects until the demand environment improves or Ginkgo can demonstrate that its newer products have broad appeal.

Market Conditions

Ginkgo continues to face a challenging demand environment, which largely stems from the rapid rise in interest rates in 2022. Funding for academic customers is also under pressure currently, although this doesn't appear to be a particularly important vertical for Ginkgo. While biopharma spend has been soft in recent years, this customer segment has actually been one of the few bright spots for Ginkgo. The company's industrial and consumer businesses appear to have come to almost a complete halt, though.

Ginkgo Bioworks Business Updates

Ginkgo's Cell Engineering business continues to shift towards projects that are smaller in scope, driven by its automation, AI, and datapoints products. While Ginkgo's Research Solutions remain the core of the business, it has been deemphasized somewhat in recent years.

Ginkgo had 102 revenue-generating Cell Engineering programs in the third quarter, a decline of 5% YoY, with Ginkgo suggesting that this was due to program rationalization. The decline is concerning, though, given Ginkgo's shift in focus towards programs that are smaller in scope. There is an ongoing transition towards biopharma and government customers, with demand from ag customers also remaining steady.

Recent program wins include a $22 million monoclonal antibody manufacturing project that was awarded by BARDA. This project aims to reduce the cost of domestically manufacturing antibodies. The project involves a number of parties, though, and the $22 million figure is a maximum contract value, so it is unclear how much revenue Ginkgo will receive. Ginkgo previously partnered with Antheia on the domestic production of active pharmaceutical ingredients. While Antheia's business continues to progress, there haven't been any partnership updates since the initial announcement.

Ginkgo and Bayer also recently extended their agriculture partnership, which primarily relates to engineering microbes. This includes nitrogen-fixing biologicals, which could be extremely valuable if successful. Ginkgo has been working on this since at least 2021, and it is not clear how much progress has been made or how close a product is to market. Pivot Bio already has a nitrogen-fixing bacteria on the market for corn. This is a supplement to synthetic fertilizer and manure rather than a replacement. As a private company, limited information is available about Pivot Bio and its financial performance. The company generated over $100 million in revenue in 2023, up around 60% from the prior year. While this is a positive in terms of end-market potential, it is concerning that Ginkgo isn't leading, as its scale and automation were supposed to enable it to dominate these types of opportunities.

Ginkgo's newer offerings include Datapoints, Automation, and Reagents. While these solutions were designed to drive adoption of Ginkgo's platform, it isn't clear what financial impact they are having. Progress appears modest given that these products have been on the market for around 18 months and Ginkgo's Cell Engineering revenue has been fairly flat over that time frame. The company's revenue mix in 2030 is expected to be something like 80-20 tools versus services, which indicates a dramatic shift in mix is expected, driven by the rapid growth of the Tools business.

Financial Analysis

Cell Engineering revenue in Q3 was 29 million USD, down 61% YoY. This isn’t as bad as it appears, as the third quarter of 2024 included $45 million of non-cash revenue, which was related to the termination of Ginkgo’s agreement with Motif Foodworks, a relationship that didn’t yield any real value for either party (Motif went bankrupt). Interestingly, legal troubles were part of Motif's downfall, which relates to claims that it infringed on the intellectual property of Impossible Foods. There is also evidence that Cronos Group ran into similar issues and was forced to license IP from Aurora and 22nd Century Group as a result. Biosecurity revenue totaled $9 million in the third quarter at a 19% gross margin (excludes SBC).

Ginkgo is now guiding to $167-$187 million in revenue in 2025, with $117-$137 million in Cell Engineering revenue and upwards of $40 million in Biosecurity revenue. This guidance suggests modest sequential growth in Q4 but a further YoY decline.

Ginkgo's adjusted EBITDA loss in the third quarter was $56 million. This is somewhat deceptive due to a $21 million shortfall related to Ginkgo's AI partnership with Google Cloud. This contract has now been amended, reducing future commitments and extending the relevant period. This was initially a 5-year partnership that was announced in the middle of 2023. It is worth keeping in mind that there was a lot of buzz around this partnership when it was announced, with investors seemingly not recognizing that Ginkgo was signing up for services from Google. Rather than creating an inflection point in Ginkgo's business or indicating a strategic interest from Google, the relationship has resulted in unnecessary expenses.

Cash burn in Q3 totaled $28 million, with the YoY reduction driven by Ginkgo's restructuring efforts. While Ginkgo still has work to do, its business is rapidly becoming viable from a cash burn perspective. It may be difficult for Ginkgo to cut its cash costs much further, which is partly due to unused real estate that the company is currently stuck with.

The shift in the nature of Ginkgo's business over the past few years also means that losses are now less acceptable. Ginkgo's previous focus on participating in downstream value meant that there was a mismatch between when costs were incurred and revenue recognized. This timing discrepancy no longer exists, though. The shift presumably should also mean that more Cell Engineering revenue is being recognized upfront, making the lack of growth even more damning.

Ginkgo had around $462 million in cash and cash equivalents at the end of the third quarter, which is a relatively large balance relative to the current burn rate. Despite this, Ginkgo recently entered into a $100 million equity sales deal, suggesting the company believes it will continue to burn cash many years into the future.

Conclusion

There is not much to like about Ginkgo's business at the moment, and the narrative supporting the stock continues to weaken. While Ginkgo's attempt to become the AWS of biology has largely been forgotten, it is worthwhile keeping in mind that there don't appear to have been any meaningfully successful commercial products to come out of the platform. This doesn't paint the capabilities of the company in the best light and suggests a reason that Ginkgo has struggled to build demand.

Considerable blame for the current situation can likely be placed on macro headwinds, but biopharma sentiment appears to have improved in recent months, and Ginkgo hasn't noted any boost from this. This stands in contrast to the likes of Twist Bioscience Corporation (TWST), with Twist quite positive on the near-term prospects of its Biopharma segment due to demand from AI-enabled drug discovery customers. Ginkgo should be exposed to this tailwind as it has invested in antibody developability capabilities.

Much of Ginkgo's value proposition now appears to be based around assets acquired from Zymergen, with Ginkgo's Foundry business reduced to a CRO. As a commodity supplier of lab services offering modest growth, Ginkgo should probably trade on a lower revenue multiple than it currently does, particularly when considering cash burn and dilution. As a result, I expect continued losses and weak growth to further pressure the share price.
